speaker: interviewer
text: '95, an interview with Eva Bergmann. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] You mentioned the nonchalant attitude of the non-Jews in Berlin when they saw people disappearing, et cetera. Could you give us a picture of the Jewish attitude during these years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. All of the, or many of the friends of my father and mother, who were Jewish and did not get a visa yet to emigrate, kept together very closely. They were able to come yet together, and, of course, they lived under constant fear, just like my parents. And they were discussing what could one do. Now, we were actually very lucky in that way, that my father knew Professor Einstein in Princeton, who had emigrated quite a long time before, in '33, and who had written to the Consulate. Many of the people did not have that, and were eventually taken to concentration camps. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But due to the fear and constant isolation one lived in Berlin, there was another problem which arised and that was I was very upset about the whole condition there, and became, physically as well as emotionally, ill. And didn't want to-- I couldn't work any more, I didn't want to eat, I couldn't sleep, and I just wanted to get out of Germany. And the main thing was my condition-- you see, I needed a certificate of good health, which was to accompany the visa, but I couldn't get it. So for a long time, my parents asked some doctors with whom I was in treatment to write out a certificate of good health. And there was a very old, old doctor-- I think he was over 90 years-- whom my father's father knew already. And he wrote out a certificate of good health, which was required for the visa. My parents finally got their visum in September 1940. And at that time, my-- but I couldn't get it. But at that time, my brother tried to get out. He went on a so-called transit visa, which was without quota number. This was a non-quota transit visa to England where he stayed and he worked there for a while. But when France collapsed, he was caught and they thought he was a German spy. They caught him and they put him into a camp, just like my husband, Fred. By the way, they met later on. And Hans was interned-- I do not know, I think a year and a half. And then later on-- he was shipped to Canada, you see. He was interned in Canada. And later on, when he got out, he was in Canada, in Ottawa, and worked in the Department of National Defense for a while. And there-- and Hans is still alive. He is two years older than I and he worked until two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at the time when Hans made it to England and eventually to Canada, was he able to communicate with his parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was not able when he was-- he was able to communicate while he was in England still, but not while he was in camp. It was not allowed when he was interned. This was not a real concentration camp, but when he was interned, he was not allowed to write to his parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: So actually, we didn't even know where he was. We did not even know that he was interned. We knew that he was in England. And only much later on, of course, from Ottawa, we heard that he was released from camp and that he worked in the Department of National Defense. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And my parents now tried to get my visa. And I remember one day when I went with my mother to the American Consulate in Berlin in the hope that a miracle would happen and they would give it to me. And I will never forget how we were treated there. They told us right away-- that was the American Consulate-- there's no hope. For the time being, you will not have a visa nor quota number. There's no hope that you'll come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came out of that-- of-- out of the building, and on each side of the building was Gestapo laughing at us and saying, you Jews, why do you even try here? And we hurried home. At that time, I was quite sick already, and was weak. And I know we hurried to the bus-- we were still allowed to go in the bus-- to go home. But I couldn't run very well, and my mother had to drag me to the bus. But when I came home, of course, and told Dad, he was also very desolated. Well, what are we going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, as I said before, a-- a doctor wrote out a certificate of good health, which was the beginning that I got the visa. And I went on a non-quota visa to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to backtrack and mention Kristallnacht, which I am sure-- I assume you have some memories of Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you do, will you share them with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Kristallnacht, we were-- that was at night when it began, way at night, after 11:00 or 12:00. We were already in bed. Now-- just a minute-- my brother had emigrated already, I think. No, my brother was still there. And we heard, all of a sudden, loud clattering in the street. And we heard that it was glass, shattering of glass. And voices that were screaming, people were screaming. But we didn't dare to look out of the window. We were afraid. Only the next morning, we looked down and we saw on the street. We had a lot of Jewish groceries and a tailor and carpenter-- they were all Jewish around us. And we saw that the glass was lying on the street. People tried to sweep the glass away. And we were horrified. And we went-- later on, much later-- around 5:00, we went out and we saw the nearby synagogue. I could not tell you the name of the synagogue, which was owned by [INAUDIBLE] in shatter. The-- the glass were all-- was all taken out, and on the-- in the yard were the prayer books, torn up, and there were ashes all over. It was a horrible look. The-- the-- the temple-- the synagogue was hardly recognizable. And even though Mom, Dad, nor Hans nor I ever went to the synagogue, but what a horrible experience for the Jewish people. And we only saw that some people were cleaning up the ashes in that-- in that courtyard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were those people who were cleaning it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- yeah, I do think they engaged Jewish people, not the Christian, because I have read that some Jewish people later on had to be on their knees cleaning up the-- the glasses and all. I had read that in one of the books.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is all pre-war. The war is not yet on.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what happened between Kristallnacht and 1939, September, when war broke out? What happens to the Bergmann family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, when the-- yeah, when the war broke out, of course, we were in the cellar, in the Luftschutzkeller.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was '39. We had already-- every time when we heard the sirens-- and I still, in memory, hear the sirens blowing. It was a terrible sound. We were always packed. We had a big suitcase and little bags, which we were supposed to take down with some food and the most necessary things, because we knew that, in the Luftschutzkeller, we had to sit for a long time. And we-- Mum and Dad and I do not remember that Hans was there. That I do not remember. But we were running down in the cellar, and we had to cross the yard, and that was a terrible experience because we heard the bombs falling. And we had to run over to the other side of the house in order to get to the Luftschutzkeller.

speaker: interviewer
text: Luftschutzkeller means the air raid shelter.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the air raid shelter. Yeah. And then there, we were segregated. The Jews on one side, the Christian on the other. As I was sick, I had a sort of a little armchair on which I lay, because I wasn't well enough to sit the whole night. And we had pillows and all. And I remember one funny story, really. There was a cat in the Christian shelter. And that cat ran from the Christian shelter to the Jewish, and the Christian people were very angry and hit the cat. You do not go to Jews, they used to say. [LAUGHS] And then during the morning, I was-- we sat there often from, well, 10:00 on, hearing the bombs outside. We heard the bombs in the air raid shelter. And we sat there until the morning, sometimes 5:00 in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that happen often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, quite often. Yeah. And it was terrible, fearful. And I am-- I was more afraid of the sirens, which one heard and one immediately had to take the suitcases and everything you had and run downstairs. You couldn't lose a minute. And I don't think we had an elevator. I think we ran down the stairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned an aunt, Aunt Gertrude. Would you like to tell us about her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Aunt Gertrude was the sister of my mother. She was a photographer and she was still in Germany until-- I have a picture, which she took from me in 1937, still. And then she went to Holland and lived there underground until the Nazis found her in '41 and deported her to Theresienstadt. Now in-- at that time, she found my grandma there still, who was still in Theresienstadt. She found her very ill and undernourished, but she took care of her, physically, for a while, until my grandmother died. Then my aunt was deported to Auschwitz-- at that time, Auschwitz. And there she was what they called [? Glimmerwork. ?] I do not really know what it was. It was a work which was supposed to be done in a very fast way, and she did not-- was not a fast person. And they took her and put her into the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that she-- in Holland, that Aunt Gertrude was in the underground.

speaker: interviewee
text: Underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she working with underground with a resistance group or she just went into hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: She went into hiding. She was not working with a resistance group. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any idea how she was discovered by the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was discovered by the Nazis because they searched, of course, everywhere, just like they did with Anne Frank. And they found her one day and-- and asked her questions. Why are you here? You are a Jew. And they deported her immediately to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when war breaks out, what happens to the still non-deported Berlin Jewry?

speaker: interviewee
text: They tried to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] live a-- a desolated life. It must have been terrible for people who knew they wouldn't get out anymore. And we were actually in the same boat, because we did not see will we ever get the visum after our exper-- well, after my experience with my mother where we went to the American Consulate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were desperated, and we couldn't get together any more so much. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we tried to live a life where we, at least-- we read a lot and my grandmother was very positive. My grandmother, the mother of my mother, with whom we lived that I want--

speaker: interviewee
text: Charolotte.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, to tell you, was a very positive person. And she never thought, these things could never happen to us. She even went outside and sat on benches which were not marked for Jew. And funny enough, she was not caught. But she was like that. She was never afraid. That can never happen. When it really came to the point where she heard that so many people were deported, then she-- only her eyes opened. But she was very hopeful that we would still get a visa for her once we were in America. And when we-- I remember when we separated from her on the day of our emigration, in April '41, she said goodbye to us and said, don't worry. You will get the visa for me very soon, and I will see you. And our Nana was there, too, and she said the same. She said, you will get the visa for her and I will take care of Grandma until-- until she cannot live any more here, but I will be with her all the time and I will try to bring her food. Nana was absolutely wonderful person.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Professor Einstein's letter to the US Consulate, I assume, facilitated, eventually, getting of visas?

speaker: interviewee
text: It helped. You see, he requested that the visa would be sent-- it was an urgent request. And he said, they must get out of Germany. And he also gave a letter about my father, whom he knew and with whom he had worked for so many years in Berlin, telling of his very good contact and what a wonderful physicist he was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said in that letter that it was very urgent that we leave because I was sick and would only find health again in a new country where one wasn't under oppression. It did help in a way, but I think the main thing that helped was that letter from the old doctor who had written a letter of good health that was accompanying the-- you see, there was a letter of good health and of good-- and of good-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that one was not a robber or anything, you know? Character.

speaker: interviewee
text: Character, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened when you finally did get the visa approved?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I finally got the visa, that was in February, '41. February, '41, which was really the last minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had already, before that, packed quite a few things which we were allowed to take. Many of the things we were not allowed to take and leave-- left it with my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of things were you not allowed to take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not allowed-- for instance, we were not allowed to take any jewelry. We didn't have much jewelry. No cutlery-- silver cutlery. That was all staying in Germany. And, yeah, glasses and things we were allowed. And we were not allowed-- the furniture, of course, was staying behind, which we didn't care. You see, we were so eager to get out now, finally, that we didn't care what we left in Germany, except my poor grandma. And then, when we came to America, of course, we tried urgently to get my grandmother over, to get a visa. We couldn't. We never were able to get her one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you arrived in the United States in the year of--

speaker: interviewee
text: April-- April 15, 1941. And we went via [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Germany for, I think, 36 hours through Germany and through France-- occupied France, occupied with the-- the trains were all darkened. And Spain and Portugal. We went from Portugal, Lisbon, on the which was a big ship which took us. But we were-- the-- the trains were darkened because of the-- we were not allowed to see any military significance in-- in France, you see. So we didn't get any food until much, much later in the train, but that was all right. And we went with a-- the whole emigration was arranged by a Jewish organization, which was called HIAS. H-I-A-S. That was Hebrew Immigrant Aid Society. And they arranged the whole trip for us. And when we came to Spain, we stayed in Spain for a day. It was beautiful in-- in Spain. We liked it there. And then we went on, the next day, to Portugal, Lisbon, where we entered the yeah, the ship. Our-- our ship was very good. We-- we had a very good crossing, and we arrived here in Ellis Island. And there we were questioned for a whole day. Now, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they questioned especially me, seeing that I was very sort of undernourished and not well.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they questioned me, will I be able to work? Will I not be a burden to the United States? Even though they had all the letters from several professors and people who had already emigrated before.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed there for the day. Mum and dad and I were questioned very much. And then at night we were picked up by a friend of my father, a professor who taught at the City College.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Professor [? Zumanski ?] was his name. He was a professor at City College and he took us not to-- not to New York but he took us to Princeton, where Einstein was. And there, we knew Professor [? Latenburg ?] and the whole [? Latenburg ?] family, where we lived for about, I think, two months with them. [? Latenburg ?] was a man who'd worked with Dad in Breslau, but he was able to emigrate much earlier. In fact, to my opinion, only-- he was only half Jewish, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father bring any-- any greetings or any kind of messages to the professor or his colleagues in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he did. The day before we emigrated, a very young physicist [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] was visiting Dad in our apartment in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Houterman, H-O-U-T-E-R-M-A-N. He was sent by [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another physicist, Max Von Laue. He was a German Christian, Max Von Laue, also a physicist from Berlin. And Daddy was supposed to take a very important message to American friends, saying that they are trying to hinder the work to make an atomic bomb. And Heisenberg, who was one of the very famous physicists in Berlin, was under great pressure and felt that it was almost not possible to delay the work. But he warned American friends through Daddy, please tell them to accelerate the work there if-- here, if they have done so before. And this was, of course, a message which Dad had to carry by heart, because it was too dangerous to write it down. Because if Dad would have been captured and we all would have been captured, it would have been a terrible danger, and also for Houterman himself. Yeah.
